title: Spark plug fouling and heat range selection — all makes and models
description: Spark plug fouling is the single most common ignition complaint across all motorcycle makes. Carbon fouling (dry black soot) indicates rich mixture or excessive idling. Oil fouling (wet, oily deposits) indicates worn valve seals or piston rings. A plug that's too cold for the application fouls quickly because it can't reach self-cleaning temperature (450-500C). A plug that's too hot pre-ignites and melts electrodes. Heat range selection is critical: each step in heat range changes the tip temperature by approximately 75-100C. Japanese manufacturers use opposite numbering — NGK higher numbers are colder, Denso higher numbers are hotter. Harley-Davidson twins run hotter on the rear cylinder due to reduced airflow, often requiring one step colder plug in the rear.
causes: Running too rich from fueling issues — carbon deposits short the electrode to ground
Oil consumption past worn valve stem seals or piston rings — oil coats the insulator
Spark plug heat range too cold for riding style — never reaches self-cleaning temperature
Extended idling or short-trip riding — plug never gets hot enough to burn off deposits
Incorrect plug gap — too wide for the ignition system's output voltage
fix_procedure: 1. Remove spark plug with correct socket (16mm for most Japanese bikes, 5/8" for Harleys). Use a torque wrench for reinstallation — overtightening strips aluminum heads. 2. Read the plug: tan/light brown insulator = correct mixture and heat range. Dry black soot = carbon fouled (rich or cold plug). Wet black = oil fouled (engine problem). White/blistered = too hot or too lean (dangerous). 3. For carbon fouling: first fix the fueling issue (rich mixture, stuck choke/enrichener, leaking injector). Then verify heat range — if the bike is primarily city ridden, go one step hotter. NGK CR8E → CR9E is one step COLDER. NGK CR8E → CR7E is one step HOTTER. 4. Plug gap: check with a wire-type feeler gauge, NOT a coin-type. Typical gaps: 0.6-0.7mm for CDI ignitions, 0.7-0.9mm for coil-on-plug systems. Iridium and platinum plugs should NEVER be re-gapped — the fine electrode will break. 5. Torque specs: 10-12 ft-lb for 12mm plugs in aluminum heads, 15-20 ft-lb for 14mm plugs. Without a torque wrench: finger-tight plus 1/4 turn for a new gasket plug, 1/8 turn for a used plug. 6. Forum tip: spark plug fouling is a symptom, not a root cause. Throwing new plugs at a fouling problem is a waste of money — find why it's fouling first. The most common mistake is running a plug that's too cold for the riding style. A commuter bike that never sees 6000 RPM doesn't need the same plug as a track bike. NGK's cross-reference chart (ngk.com) is the definitive source for heat range equivalents across brands. For Harley twins, the rear cylinder runs 50-100F hotter than the front — some mechanics run one step colder in the rear (NGK DCPR7E front, DCPR8E rear on Twin Cams) to equalize plug life. Iridium plugs (NGK CR9EIX, $12-15 each) last 3-4x longer than copper but cost 4x more — worth it on bikes where plug access is difficult (e.g., Honda VFR800 rear cylinders require partial fairing removal). Always keep a spare set of plugs under the seat on any long ride.
parts_needed: Spark plugs (OEM spec — NGK or Denso, match heat range to application)
Spark plug socket (16mm thin-wall for most Japanese, 5/8" for Harley)
Wire feeler gauge set (for gap checking)
Anti-seize compound (nickel-based, thin coat on threads — NOT on iridium/platinum plugs)
Torque wrench (ft-lb range for plug installation)

title: Ignition coil failure — primary and secondary winding testing across all platforms
description: Ignition coils transform 12V battery voltage into 15,000-40,000V to fire the spark plug. They fail from heat cycling, vibration, internal insulation breakdown, and age. Stick coils (coil-on-plug, common on 2000s+ sportbikes) fail differently than remote coils (external coils with plug wires, common on cruisers and older bikes). Primary winding failure causes a dead cylinder — no spark at all. Secondary winding failure causes weak spark — runs at idle but misfires under load when the cylinder pressure is highest and requires the most voltage to ionize the gap. Internal coil cracks allow voltage to arc to ground internally, producing intermittent misfires that are maddening to diagnose without methodical testing.
causes: Internal insulation breakdown from heat cycling — secondary winding shorts between layers
Cracked coil housing from vibration or impact — moisture ingress causes arcing to ground
Primary winding open circuit — broken wire from thermal expansion/contraction cycles
Corroded coil connector pins — high resistance in primary circuit reduces output voltage
Stick coil boot deterioration — spark arcs through the boot instead of across the plug gap
fix_procedure: 1. PRIMARY WINDING TEST: Disconnect coil connector. Set multimeter to Ohms (200 ohm range). Measure resistance between the two primary terminals. Specification: 1.5-3.5 ohms for most Japanese coils, 2.5-5.0 ohms for Harley single-fire coils. An open circuit (OL) means broken primary winding — coil is dead. Significantly low resistance (<0.5 ohm) means shorted turns — coil overheats and fails intermittently. 2. SECONDARY WINDING TEST: Set multimeter to 20K ohm range. Measure between the primary positive terminal and the spark plug terminal (or plug boot contact). Specification: 6,000-15,000 ohms for most remote coils, 8,000-20,000 ohms for stick coils with integrated resistor caps. An open circuit means broken secondary winding. Very low reading (<3,000 ohms) means shorted secondary turns — weak spark under load. 3. VISUAL INSPECTION: Look for cracks in the coil housing, melted/discolored areas (hot spots from internal arcing), corroded terminals, and damaged plug boots. On stick coils: inspect the rubber boot for carbon tracking lines (gray/white streaks where voltage arced through the boot). 4. SPARK TEST: Remove plug, reconnect to coil, ground plug threads against engine. Crank engine — spark should be bright blue and snap audibly. Weak orange spark = failing coil or weak ignition source. No spark = dead coil or broken circuit upstream. 5. Forum tip: the multimeter test catches 80% of coil failures, but some coils test fine cold and fail when hot. If you suspect an intermittent coil, heat it with a heat gun (not a torch) to 150-200F and re-test resistance — a failing coil's secondary resistance will jump dramatically when hot. For Kawasaki ZX-6R/ZX-10R stick coils (part numbers 21171-0033, 21171-0034), the boot is the weak point — it cracks from heat and the spark arcs through the boot to the valve cover instead of through the plug. Replacement boots alone are $8-12 versus $45-65 for the whole stick coil. On Harley Twin Cams with dual-fire coils, both plugs fire simultaneously — if one plug wire or coil tower is bad, BOTH cylinders lose spark. Single-fire coil upgrades ($80-120, Dynatek or Accel) give each cylinder its own coil and improve spark energy by 40%. For any multi-cylinder bike with one dead cylinder: swap the suspect coil with a known-good coil from another cylinder. If the dead cylinder follows the coil, the coil is confirmed bad. This takes 5 minutes and is more conclusive than any resistance test.
parts_needed: Ignition coil (OEM or quality aftermarket — Denso, NGK, Dynatek)
Stick coil boots (if boot is the failure point — $8-12 each)
Dielectric grease (for plug boots and coil connections)
Multimeter (for resistance testing)
Heat gun (for hot-test diagnosis of intermittent failures)

title: CDI/ECU ignition module failure — testing without an oscilloscope
description: The CDI (Capacitor Discharge Ignition) or ECU ignition module is the brain of the ignition system. CDI units are common on pre-2000 bikes and smaller displacement machines — they store energy in a capacitor and discharge it all at once for a fast, high-voltage spark. ECU-controlled ignitions (transistor-switched or digital) are standard on modern fuel-injected bikes. Both fail from heat, vibration, moisture intrusion, and voltage spikes. Diagnosing a failed ignition module without an oscilloscope requires methodical elimination of every other component first — the module should be the LAST thing you condemn because it's the most expensive and can't be bench-tested with basic tools.
causes: Internal semiconductor failure from heat cycling — module works cold, fails when hot
Moisture intrusion through cracked housing or corroded connector — shorts internal circuits
Voltage spikes from jump-starting or bad voltage regulator — destroys transistors
Vibration cracking solder joints on internal circuit board — intermittent connection
Ground wire corrosion — module can't complete its circuit and fires erratically or not at all
fix_procedure: 1. CRITICAL RULE: Never condemn the CDI/ECU until you've tested EVERY other ignition component first. The elimination sequence is: battery voltage (12.5V+) → kill switch circuit → fuse → ignition switch → sidestand/clutch interlocks → pickup coil resistance → trigger coil air gap → ignition coil primary/secondary resistance → plug wires/caps → spark plugs. If ALL of those test good and there's still no spark, THEN suspect the module. 2. BATTERY VOLTAGE TEST: CDI units need minimum 10.5V to fire; ECU ignitions need 11V+. A weak battery that cranks the engine slowly may not provide enough voltage for the ignition module. Charge battery fully before any ignition diagnosis. 3. CONNECTOR INSPECTION: Unplug the CDI/ECU connector. Inspect every pin for corrosion (green/white deposits), bent pins, or backed-out terminals. Clean with electrical contact cleaner and a small brass brush. Re-seat firmly. This fixes 30% of 'bad CDI' diagnoses. 4. GROUND TEST: Verify the module's ground wire has less than 0.5 ohm resistance to the frame/engine ground point. Corroded grounds cause more ignition problems than failed modules. 5. HEAT TEST: If the bike starts cold and dies after 10-20 minutes (or won't restart when hot), the module is likely failing when it reaches operating temperature. To confirm: when it dies, immediately cool the module with compressed air or a cold rag. If it restarts, the module is heat-sensitive and needs replacement. 6. SUBSTITUTION TEST: The most definitive test. Borrow or buy a known-good module for your exact model and swap it. If the bike runs perfectly, the original module is bad. 7. Forum tip: 'bad CDI' is the most over-diagnosed problem in motorcycle repair. It's become a catch-all for 'I can't figure out what's wrong.' In reality, CDI/ECU failure is uncommon compared to bad connections, corroded grounds, failed pickup coils, and weak batteries. The heat-soak test is the most useful diagnostic: if the bike dies after 15-20 minutes and restarts after cooling the CDI box specifically, you've found your problem. For older Suzuki GSX-R models (1986-1995), aftermarket CDI units from Rick's Motorsport Electrics ($150-250) are more reliable than NOS Suzuki units because they use modern components. For any bike: ALWAYS check the voltage regulator/rectifier output before replacing a CDI — a bad reg/rec that outputs voltage spikes will destroy a new CDI immediately. Fix the reg/rec first.
parts_needed: CDI/ECU module (OEM or Rick's Motorsport Electrics aftermarket)
Electrical contact cleaner (for connector pins)
Multimeter (for voltage, resistance, and ground testing)
Compressed air canister (for heat-soak cooling test)
Dielectric grease (for connector waterproofing after service)

title: Pickup coil (CKP sensor) — resistance testing and air gap adjustment
description: The pickup coil (also called CKP sensor, pulser coil, or trigger coil) tells the ignition module when to fire. It sits near a toothed reluctor wheel on the crankshaft or camshaft and generates an AC voltage pulse as each tooth passes. If the pickup coil fails, the ignition module never receives the fire command — result is no spark on all cylinders simultaneously. Unlike a single coil failure (one dead cylinder), a pickup coil failure kills ALL cylinders at once. The air gap between the pickup coil tip and the reluctor teeth is critical: too wide and the signal is too weak, too narrow and the coil tip contacts the spinning reluctor and is destroyed.
causes: Internal winding break from heat and vibration — open circuit, no signal generated
Insulation breakdown between windings — shorted turns reduce signal amplitude below CDI threshold
Air gap too wide from mounting bolt loosening or gasket stack-up — signal too weak at cranking RPM
Reluctor wheel damage — missing or chipped teeth create erratic signal pattern
Connector corrosion — high-resistance connection attenuates the already-small AC signal
fix_procedure: 1. RESISTANCE TEST: Disconnect the pickup coil connector at the CDI/ECU harness. Set multimeter to Ohms (2K range). Measure between the two pickup coil wires. Specification varies by make: Honda = 100-500 ohms typically, Kawasaki = 380-580 ohms typically, Suzuki = 100-300 ohms typically, Yamaha = 248-372 ohms typically, Harley (2000+ Delphi) = 900-1100 ohms. An open circuit (OL) means the coil is dead. Significantly low resistance suggests shorted turns. Test at room temperature AND hot (after running) — some coils fail only when hot. 2. AC VOLTAGE OUTPUT TEST (best test): Set multimeter to AC Volts. Leave pickup coil connected. Crank the engine. The pickup coil should generate at least 0.5V AC at cranking speed (200 RPM). At idle it should produce 2-5V AC. If the resistance is in spec but the voltage output is low, the air gap is too wide or the reluctor is damaged. 3. AIR GAP CHECK: The gap between the pickup coil tip and the reluctor teeth should be 0.2-0.6mm (0.008-0.024 inches) for most applications. Use a non-magnetic feeler gauge (brass or plastic). Rotate the engine to align a reluctor tooth with the pickup coil tip, then measure. Adjust by loosening the pickup coil mounting screws and shimming or repositioning. 4. WIRING INSPECTION: Trace the pickup coil wires from the sensor through the engine case to the connector. These wires pass through a harsh environment (oil, heat, vibration) and chafe against the engine case. Look for rubbed-through insulation, pinched wires, and corroded connectors. 5. Forum tip: the pickup coil is the most commonly missed cause of a complete no-spark condition. Mechanics jump straight to 'bad CDI' when the real problem is a $30-60 pickup coil. The key diagnostic clue: if ALL cylinders lose spark simultaneously, it's almost never the coils (they don't all fail at once) — it's the pickup coil, CDI, or kill switch circuit. The AC voltage test while cranking is more conclusive than the resistance test because it verifies the coil actually generates a signal, not just that the wire is intact. For Honda VTX1300/1800 and Shadow models, the pickup coil is inside the left engine cover and requires removing the stator cover to access — budget 1.5 hours for access alone. For inline-4 sportbikes, the pickup coil is usually externally accessible near the alternator cover. Always inspect the reluctor wheel teeth while you have access — a chipped tooth creates an intermittent misfire that's nearly impossible to diagnose any other way.
parts_needed: Pickup coil / CKP sensor (OEM recommended — critical tolerances)
Non-magnetic feeler gauge set (brass or plastic, 0.008-0.024")
Multimeter (for resistance and AC voltage tests)
Gasket for stator/pickup cover (required if removing the cover)
Electrical contact cleaner and dielectric grease (for connectors)

title: Spark plug wire and cap resistance — NGK standards and testing
description: Spark plug wires and caps carry 15,000-40,000 volts from the ignition coil to the spark plug. They use a carbon-core or wire-wound resistor core to suppress radio frequency interference (RFI). Over time, the carbon core breaks down and resistance increases — eventually the wire can't deliver enough voltage to fire the plug under load. Resistor caps (NGK types XB05F, XD05F, XZ05F, etc.) add 5K ohms of resistance for additional RFI suppression. A bad cap or wire is one of the most common and cheapest causes of a misfire, yet it's frequently overlooked because mechanics assume the wire is just a wire.
causes: Carbon core degradation from heat cycling — resistance increases beyond specification
Plug cap internal resistor failure — open circuit or excessive resistance
Wire insulation cracking from heat and ozone exposure — voltage arcs to ground through the crack
Poor cap-to-plug connection — corroded or loose terminal reduces energy transfer
Wire-to-coil connection loosened from vibration — intermittent high-resistance contact
fix_procedure: 1. WIRE RESISTANCE TEST: Remove plug wire from both the coil and the spark plug. Set multimeter to 20K ohm range. Measure end-to-end resistance. NGK standard: wire resistance should be less than 12,000 ohms per foot of wire length (approximately 4,000 ohms per 30cm). Typical plug wire is 12-18 inches, so total wire resistance should be 12,000-25,000 ohms. If resistance exceeds 25,000 ohms per foot, the carbon core has degraded — replace the wire. An open circuit (OL) means the core is broken — wire is dead. 2. CAP RESISTANCE TEST: Remove the plug cap from the wire. Measure resistance across the cap alone. NGK standard: resistor caps should be 5,000 ohms (+/- 20%). A cap reading over 8,000 ohms or showing OL is failed and must be replaced. Non-resistor caps should read near 0 ohms. 3. TOTAL SYSTEM RESISTANCE: wire + cap combined should be under 25,000 ohms total for most applications. Above this, the ignition system may not have enough voltage to fire the plug under high-RPM compression loads. 4. VISUAL INSPECTION: Look for cracks, melting, carbon tracks (gray/white lines on the outside where voltage has been arcing), and loose connections. Wiggle each connection while the engine idles — if the idle changes, you've found a bad connection. 5. REPLACEMENT: NGK plug caps screw onto the wire — the wire must be cut square and the cap threaded on. OEM wires are typically silicone-jacketed; aftermarket copper-core racing wires have lower resistance but higher RFI. For street bikes, OEM-style carbon core is preferred. 6. Forum tip: spark plug wires and caps are the cheapest ignition components ($5-15 per wire, $4-8 per cap) and the most overlooked. Any bike over 10 years old with original plug wires should have them replaced proactively. The classic symptom of a high-resistance wire is a misfire that only appears under load (highway speed, uphill, two-up riding) because cylinder pressure is highest and requires the most voltage to jump the plug gap. At idle, even a marginal wire may work because cylinder pressure is low. For Kawasaki Vulcan 900/1500/2000 and other V-twin cruisers, the rear cylinder plug wire runs close to the rear exhaust header and cooks from radiant heat — it fails first. Check it twice as often as the front. NGK cap part numbers: XB05F (90-degree, resistor, 5K ohm), XD05F (straight, resistor, 5K ohm), LB05F (90-degree, resistor, 5K ohm, SAE terminal). Always match the cap angle to the original — a straight cap on a plug that needs a 90-degree cap will stress the wire and break the connection.
parts_needed: NGK spark plug wires (silicone-jacketed, carbon core, correct length per cylinder)
NGK plug caps (XB05F, XD05F, or LB05F — match angle and terminal type to application)
Multimeter (for resistance testing)
Wire stripper/cutter (for clean wire ends before threading caps)
Dielectric grease (for cap-to-plug connection — prevents corrosion and moisture)

title: Points ignition maintenance and electronic conversion — vintage bikes
description: Motorcycles built before 1980 (and some into the mid-1980s) use mechanical breaker points to trigger the ignition. Points are a wear item: the rubbing block wears against the cam lobe, changing the dwell angle and timing; the contact faces pit and burn from the electrical arc each time they open; and the point gap closes as the rubbing block wears, retarding the timing and eventually preventing spark entirely. Electronic ignition conversion kits (Dynatek Dyna-S, Boyer Bransden, Pamco) replace the points and condenser with a magnetic trigger and electronic module, eliminating all points maintenance. This is the single most impactful upgrade for any points-ignition vintage motorcycle.
causes: Rubbing block wear on cam lobe — point gap closes, timing retards, eventually no spark
Contact face pitting and burning — increased resistance across points reduces primary current
Condenser failure — points arc excessively, rapid pitting and failure
Point spring fatigue — points bounce at high RPM, causing misfire above 5000 RPM
Cam lobe wear — inconsistent point opening causes uneven timing between cylinders
fix_procedure: 1. POINT GAP INSPECTION AND ADJUSTMENT: Remove points cover. Rotate engine until the rubbing block is on the peak of the cam lobe (maximum point opening). Measure gap with a feeler gauge — specification is typically 0.012-0.016 inches (0.3-0.4mm) for most Japanese bikes, 0.018 inches for Harley Shovelheads and Ironheads. Adjust by loosening the point plate screw and moving the fixed contact. 2. CONTACT INSPECTION: Points should have clean, flat, gray contact faces. If pitted (tiny craters) or burned (black deposits), they need replacement. A point file can resurface mildly pitted contacts as a field repair, but replacement is the real fix. 3. CONDENSER TEST: The condenser absorbs the voltage spike when points open. A failed condenser causes blue arcing across the points (visible with the cover off in a dark garage) and rapid point destruction. Test with a capacitance meter: should read 0.18-0.25 microfarads for most applications. Replace the condenser with every point set — they're $8-12 and fail without warning. 4. TIMING VERIFICATION: After setting point gap, verify timing with a timing light (dynamic) or test light (static). Static timing: connect a test light across the points, rotate engine to the timing mark, points should JUST open (light comes on) at the mark. 5. ELECTRONIC CONVERSION (recommended for all points bikes): Dynatek Dyna-S ($100-130) or Pamco ($80-120) kits replace points, condenser, and advance mechanism with a hall-effect trigger and electronic module. Installation is 1-2 hours. Benefits: no more point gap maintenance, no timing drift, no condenser failure, cleaner spark at all RPM, no point bounce at high RPM. The conversion fits inside the original points cover — the bike looks stock externally. 6. Forum tip: if you own a points-ignition motorcycle and ride it regularly, convert to electronic ignition. The $100-130 for a Dyna-S pays for itself in one season of NOT adjusting points every 3,000 miles. The performance improvement is immediately noticeable: easier starting, smoother idle, stronger high-RPM pull, and zero maintenance. For Yamaha XS650s specifically, the Pamco electronic ignition ($80) is the most popular conversion — it fits inside the stock points cover and includes a new advance mechanism. For British twins (Triumph, BSA, Norton), Boyer Bransden ($120-150) is the gold standard. For Harley Shovelheads and Ironheads, the Dyna-S DS1-2 kit ($130) eliminates the points and mechanical advance. After any electronic conversion, verify timing with a strobe light at idle and at 3000 RPM to confirm the electronic advance curve matches the original specification.
parts_needed: Breaker points and condenser set (for maintenance path — one per cylinder)
Feeler gauge set (for point gap, 0.012-0.018" range)
Point file (for emergency field resurfacing)
Dyna-S, Pamco, or Boyer Bransden electronic ignition kit (for conversion — model-specific)
Timing light (strobe type, for dynamic timing verification)

title: Ignition timing — static and dynamic verification across all ignition types
description: Ignition timing determines when the spark fires relative to the piston position. Correct timing is critical: too advanced causes detonation (pinging), engine damage, and overheating; too retarded causes power loss, poor fuel economy, and overheating from late combustion. On points bikes, timing drifts as points wear. On electronic bikes, timing is fixed by the CDI/ECU but can still be wrong if the pickup coil is misaligned, the woodruff key on the flywheel is sheared, or someone previously adjusted timing incorrectly. Verification requires understanding both static timing (engine not running, checking alignment of marks) and dynamic timing (engine running, using a strobe timing light).
causes: Points wear closing the gap — timing retards progressively
Flywheel woodruff key partially sheared from backfire — timing jumps several degrees
Pickup coil mounting loosened from vibration — timing shifts from correct position
Previous mechanic set timing incorrectly after service
Mechanical advance mechanism stuck from corrosion — full advance not reached or stuck in full advance
fix_procedure: 1. STATIC TIMING CHECK (all bikes): Locate the timing marks on the flywheel or alternator rotor — visible through an inspection window or by removing a timing cover plug. Rotate the engine slowly (by hand or with spark plugs removed and rear wheel in gear) to align the 'F' or timing mark with the fixed reference pointer. For points bikes: connect a test light across the points — the light should illuminate exactly as the mark aligns (points just opening). For electronic bikes: the pickup coil should be aligned with the reluctor tooth at this position. 2. DYNAMIC TIMING CHECK (all bikes): Connect an inductive timing light to the #1 cylinder plug wire. Start the engine and idle. Point the strobe at the timing marks — the strobe freezes the flywheel position visually. The timing mark should align with the reference at idle. Increase RPM to 3000-4000 — the mark should advance (move in the direction of rotation) as the mechanical or electronic advance kicks in. Full advance specification varies: typically 28-35 degrees BTDC for Japanese inline-4s, 25-32 degrees for V-twins, 35-40 degrees for Harley Big Twins. 3. MECHANICAL ADVANCE CHECK: On bikes with mechanical advance weights (centrifugal advance), the mechanism can stick from corrosion, causing the timing to be stuck retarded (no advance = massive power loss above 3000 RPM) or stuck advanced (detonation at low RPM = engine damage). Remove the advance mechanism, clean pivot posts with fine emery cloth, lubricate with light grease, and verify the weights move freely and the springs return them fully. 4. WOODRUFF KEY INSPECTION: A partially sheared flywheel woodruff key shifts timing by 10-20 degrees and is nearly impossible to diagnose without removing the flywheel/rotor. If static timing cannot be set correctly despite correct pickup/points alignment, suspect a sheared key. 5. Forum tip: on Harley Sportsters (all years with points or electronic ignition), the timing mark is visible through a plug in the left engine case. Use a 7/16" or 12mm socket to remove the timing plug. The mark 'T' is top dead center; advance marks are before it in the direction of rotation. For Twin Cam 88/96 Harleys, timing is ECU-controlled and non-adjustable — if the timing is wrong, it's a sensor issue (CKP or CMP), not a timing adjustment issue. For any bike with a mechanical advance: pour penetrating oil (PB Blaster) into the advance mechanism through the points/pickup cover every spring — this prevents the advance weights from seizing over winter storage. A stuck mechanical advance is the #1 cause of sudden performance loss on vintage bikes that were running fine before storage.
parts_needed: Timing light (inductive strobe type — Innova 5568 is $30 and works perfectly)
Test light or continuity tester (for static timing on points bikes)
Flywheel holding tool (if woodruff key inspection needed — model-specific)
Woodruff key (carry a spare — $2 part that causes a $200 diagnostic headache)
Advance mechanism springs (replace if stretched — Honda, Yamaha, Kawasaki still stock these)

title: Kill switch circuit — intermittent open creating random no-spark condition
description: The engine kill switch (run/stop switch) on the right handlebar controls is a normally-closed circuit that provides ground or power to the ignition system. When the switch corrodes internally, the contact surfaces develop oxidation that creates intermittent high-resistance connections. This causes random no-spark events: the bike runs fine, then suddenly cuts out completely (all cylinders simultaneously), then may restart immediately or after wiggling the switch. This is one of the most frustrating no-spark problems because it's intermittent, affects all cylinders simultaneously, and the kill switch is the last thing most mechanics check.
causes: Internal contact oxidation from moisture and age — creates high-resistance intermittent connection
Contact spring fatigue — insufficient pressure to maintain clean contact through vibration
Water intrusion into switch housing from rain riding or washing — accelerates corrosion
Wire terminal corrosion at the switch connector — resistance in the circuit
Physical damage to switch from drop or handlebar contact — internal mechanism misaligned
fix_procedure: 1. QUICK DIAGNOSTIC: When the bike won't start or suddenly dies, FIRST toggle the kill switch on/off 10 times rapidly. If the bike then starts or the problem resolves, the kill switch contacts are corroded. This takes 5 seconds and should be the FIRST thing you try on any sudden no-spark condition. 2. RESISTANCE TEST: Set multimeter to Ohms (200 range). Disconnect the kill switch connector from the harness. Measure resistance across the kill switch terminals with the switch in the RUN position. Should be less than 1 ohm (essentially zero). Any reading above 5 ohms is a problem. Toggle the switch while watching the meter — if the reading jumps or flickers, the contacts are intermittent. 3. VOLTAGE DROP TEST (more sensitive): With the switch connected and the ignition ON, measure DC voltage across the kill switch terminals. In the RUN position, voltage should be 0V (no drop). Any voltage reading above 0.1V means resistance in the switch is dropping voltage that should go to the ignition system. 4. DISASSEMBLY AND CLEANING: Remove the right handlebar switch housing (2-4 Phillips screws). Open carefully — small springs and detent balls may fall out. Clean the contact surfaces with electrical contact cleaner (CRC QD Electronic Cleaner) and a small brass brush. Do NOT use sandpaper — it removes the plating. Apply a thin film of dielectric grease to the contacts to prevent future oxidation. Reassemble, ensuring the detent spring and ball are seated correctly. 5. BYPASS TEST (for definitive diagnosis): If you suspect the kill switch but can't isolate it, disconnect the kill switch connector and jumper the two wires that would be connected in the RUN position. If the bike runs perfectly with the switch bypassed, the switch is confirmed bad. WARNING: Do not ride with the switch bypassed — you need a way to kill the engine in an emergency. 6. Forum tip: the kill switch is the most overlooked ignition component because mechanics assume it either works or doesn't. But the most common kill switch failure mode is INTERMITTENT — it works 95% of the time and fails randomly. On Suzuki SV650s (1999-2009), the right switch housing is notorious for water intrusion because the sealing is poor. Applying a bead of RTV silicone around the switch housing seam prevents water entry. On Kawasaki sport bikes, the kill switch connector is behind the headlight fairing and corrodes from road spray. Check the connector, not just the switch. On Harleys, the kill switch is part of the right handlebar control module — the entire module must be replaced if the switch fails ($80-150 OEM). On Japanese bikes, aftermarket replacement switch assemblies are $20-40. For any bike that dies randomly while riding with no warning: check the kill switch and its wiring BEFORE spending money on CDI units, coils, or stators.
parts_needed: Right handlebar switch assembly (if switch is beyond cleaning — $20-40 aftermarket, $80-150 OEM Harley)
CRC QD Electronic Cleaner (for contact cleaning)
Small brass brush (for contact surface cleaning)
Dielectric grease (for contact protection after cleaning)
RTV silicone (for sealing switch housing against water — Suzuki SV650 especially)

title: Misfires under load — lean condition versus ignition failure differential diagnosis
description: A misfire that only occurs under load (highway speed, uphill, two-up, hard acceleration) is one of the most challenging diagnostic scenarios because two completely different systems produce nearly identical symptoms. A lean misfire occurs when there's insufficient fuel for the cylinder volume at high airflow — the lean mixture fires inconsistently or not at all. An ignition misfire occurs when the spark energy is insufficient to reliably ignite the mixture under the high cylinder pressures that occur at wide-open throttle. Both produce a stumble/hesitation/bucking sensation under acceleration. Correct diagnosis requires a systematic approach that isolates the fuel system from the ignition system.
causes: IGNITION: High-resistance plug wire or cap — can't deliver sufficient voltage at high RPM/compression
IGNITION: Weak ignition coil secondary — fires at idle but can't overcome high-compression cylinder pressures
IGNITION: Spark plug gap too wide for the system's voltage output — misfires when gap grows under compression
LEAN: Clogged fuel injector or main jet — insufficient fuel at high airflow
LEAN: Fuel pump weak — delivers adequate pressure at idle but can't maintain volume under load
LEAN: Intake air leak — unmetered air makes mixture lean, worse at higher throttle openings
fix_procedure: 1. SPARK PLUG READING (first step): Pull all spark plugs immediately after a ride where the misfire occurred. Read the insulators: white/light gray = lean (fuel issue); tan/light brown = correct mixture (likely ignition issue); dark brown/black = rich (not lean, definitely ignition issue). This single test narrows the diagnosis to the correct system 80% of the time. 2. IGNITION ISOLATION TEST: Install brand-new spark plugs (correct heat range, correct gap). Install brand-new plug wires and caps (or new stick coil boots). If the misfire disappears, the ignition components were the problem. Total cost: $20-50 in new consumables. This is faster and cheaper than diagnostic equipment. 3. FUEL SYSTEM ISOLATION TEST: On EFI bikes, check fuel pressure with a gauge teed into the fuel line — should be 43-58 PSI (300-400 kPa) for most Japanese EFI systems. Pressure should hold steady under load (have someone blip the throttle while you watch the gauge). If pressure drops under throttle, the fuel pump is weak. On carbureted bikes, enrichen the mixture temporarily by raising the needle one clip position or going up one main jet size — if the misfire disappears, the mixture was lean. 4. SINGLE-CYLINDER ISOLATION: On multi-cylinder bikes, disconnect one cylinder at a time by pulling its plug wire/connector. If disconnecting a specific cylinder makes no change in the running quality, that cylinder was already dead — and is your misfiring cylinder. Then focus all testing on that cylinder's ignition and fuel delivery. 5. COIL SWAP TEST: Swap the suspect cylinder's coil with a known-good coil from another cylinder. If the misfire follows the coil to the new cylinder, the coil is bad. If the misfire stays on the original cylinder, the coil is good — look at fuel delivery or compression. 6. Forum tip: the spark plug reading is 80% of the diagnosis. A white insulator means lean — stop looking at ignition and fix the fueling. A correct-colored insulator with a misfire means the mixture is right but the spark can't ignite it under load — that's ignition. The most common ignition cause of load misfires is degraded plug wires and caps. A wire that reads 30,000 ohms will fire the plug at idle (low cylinder pressure) but can't fire it at full throttle (high cylinder pressure). The cheapest and fastest diagnostic move is to throw $30 of new wires, caps, and plugs at it. If that fixes it, you're done. If not, you've eliminated the consumables and can focus on coils, fuel delivery, and compression. For Kawasaki ZX-10R (2004+) with coil-on-plug, the stick coil boots are the weak point — they crack and leak spark to the valve cover. Replace all 4 boots ($32 total) before condemning a $65 stick coil.
parts_needed: Spark plugs (new, correct spec — for diagnostic plug reading and replacement)
NGK plug wires and caps (for ignition isolation test — $15-25 per cylinder)
Fuel pressure gauge with tee adapter (for EFI fuel system testing — $25-40)
Multimeter (for coil and wire resistance testing)
Clip-position tool or needle-nose pliers (for carb needle adjustment if testing lean condition)

title: Coil-on-plug (COP) system diagnostics — modern motorcycle ignition troubleshooting
description: Coil-on-plug (COP) systems mount the ignition coil directly on top of the spark plug, eliminating plug wires entirely. This design is standard on sportbikes since approximately 2000 (Honda CBR, Kawasaki ZX, Suzuki GSX-R, Yamaha YZF-R) and on modern cruisers since approximately 2010. COP systems deliver higher spark energy than remote coils because there's zero energy loss in plug wires. However, they live in the worst possible environment: directly on top of the combustion chamber, exposed to extreme heat, vibration, and thermal cycling. Failure modes include cracked insulator boots, corroded electrical connectors, internal winding failure from heat stress, and driver transistor failure in smart coils (coils with integrated igniters).
causes: COP boot cracked from heat cycling — spark arcs through the boot to the valve cover instead of across the plug gap
Connector pin corrosion from heat and moisture — high-resistance connection reduces primary current to the coil
Internal secondary winding insulation failure — coil tests fine cold but opens or shorts when hot
Driver transistor failure in smart coils — the integrated igniter transistor burns out from heat or voltage spikes
Carbon tracking inside the spark plug well — conductive carbon path allows voltage to leak to ground before reaching the plug
fix_procedure: 1. VISUAL INSPECTION (first step on COP systems): Remove each COP coil (typically one bolt and one electrical connector). Inspect the boot: look for cracks, carbon tracking lines (gray/white streaks along the boot surface), and moisture/corrosion inside the boot. Look inside the spark plug well: check for oil (valve cover gasket leak), moisture, or carbon deposits. Any contamination in the well provides a path for spark energy to leak to ground instead of firing the plug. 2. RESISTANCE TEST (same as remote coils): Primary resistance between connector pins: 1.0-3.5 ohms typically. Secondary resistance between primary pin and boot contact: 8,000-20,000 ohms typically. An open circuit on either winding means the coil is dead. Very low secondary resistance (<5,000 ohms) means shorted secondary turns. 3. SWAP TEST (most definitive): Swap the suspect COP coil to a different cylinder. If the misfire moves to the new cylinder, the coil is bad. If the misfire stays on the original cylinder, the coil is good — check spark plug, injector, compression, and plug well contamination. This takes 5 minutes on most sportbikes and is the fastest diagnosis available. 4. BOOT REPLACEMENT: Many COP coil failures are actually boot failures. The silicone boot is replaceable separately on most applications. OEM boots: $8-15 each. Remove the old boot by twisting and pulling (they're friction-fit). Apply a thin coat of dielectric grease to the new boot before installation — this prevents sticking, aids future removal, and seals against moisture. 5. PLUG WELL CLEANING: If oil is found in the spark plug well, the valve cover gasket and/or spark plug tube seals are leaking. On inline-4 engines (CBR, ZX, GSX-R, R1), the tube seals are integral to the valve cover gasket — replace the entire gasket set ($30-60). On V-twins, each well has a separate seal. Oil in the well doesn't cause immediate failure but creates a carbon tracking path over time that eventually shorts the spark to ground. 6. SMART COIL DIAGNOSTICS (coils with integrated igniters): These coils receive a low-current trigger signal from the ECU rather than switched 12V from a separate igniter module. They can't be tested with a simple resistance check on the trigger circuit. If the coil has 3 or more pins (power, ground, trigger signal), it's a smart coil. Diagnosis is limited to swap testing and checking for trigger signal presence with a test light or noid light on the signal pin while cranking. 7. Forum tip: on Suzuki GSX-R1000 (2001-2008, K1-K8 generations), the COP coils are Denso units with a history of boot failure. The boots crack around the 30,000-40,000 mile mark and spark arcs to the cylinder head. The symptom is a misfire that appears at 5,000+ RPM under load and is absent at idle. Denso replacement boots (part 129700-4740 boot) are $10 each versus $55-70 for a complete coil. Replace all 4 boots together. On Honda CBR600RR/1000RR (2003+), the COP coils are extremely reliable but the electrical connectors corrode from heat exposure. Clean the connector pins with electrical contact cleaner and apply dielectric grease every 20,000 miles as preventive maintenance. On Yamaha R1/R6, the COP coils share the same Denso design as Suzuki — same boot failure issue, same fix. For any COP-equipped bike: never use a pressure washer near the spark plug wells — water intrusion into the wells is the #1 cause of sudden misfire after washing. Blow the wells dry with compressed air before reinstalling the coils if you suspect moisture entry.
parts_needed: COP coil assembly (OEM Denso or Keihin — $45-70 each; buy from OEM dealer, not Amazon)
COP boots (separate replacement — $8-15 each, available from Denso or aftermarket)
Valve cover gasket with tube seals (if oil is found in spark plug wells — $30-60)
Dielectric grease (for boot-to-plug connection and connector pins)
CRC QD Electronic Cleaner (for connector pin cleaning)
Compressed air (for plug well cleaning and moisture removal)
